(born 4 June 1979) is a Romanian [football](/wiki/Association_football "Association football") manager and retired player.
[Doping case not over, says Romanian Olympic Committee](http://www.espnfc.com/europe/news/2001/0728/20010728romaniadoping.html), espn.com, 21 July 2001

**Adrian Constantin Neaga** (born 4 June 1979) is a Romanian [football](/wiki/Association_football "Association football") manager and retired player.

## Playing career

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Adrian_Neaga&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Playing career")]

### Early career

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Adrian_Neaga&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Early career")]

Neaga was born in [Pitești](/wiki/Pite%C8%99ti "Pitești").

Neaga signed his first professional contract with [Argeș Pitești](/wiki/FC_Arge%C8%99_Pite%C8%99ti "FC Argeș Pitești") in 1997. He was then playing in the same team with [Adrian Mutu](/wiki/Adrian_Mutu "Adrian Mutu"). He played three games in his first season, scoring a goal, and then played two other games in the first half of the following season, before the club decided to loan him to [Dacia Pitești](/wiki/CS_Dacia_Mioveni "CS Dacia Mioveni"), a club from the [second league](/wiki/Liga_II "Liga II"), where he played 20 games and scored 10 goals, therefore gaining the right to be part of [Argeș Pitești](/wiki/FC_Arge%C8%99_Pite%C8%99ti "FC Argeș Pitești") again.

In 2001 Neaga failed a [drug test](/wiki/Drug_test "Drug test") which had been performed at the end of a [Divizia A](/wiki/Divizia_A "Divizia A") game. He tested positive for the anabolic steroid [metenolone](/wiki/Metenolone "Metenolone").[[1]](#cite_note-1) He was not sanctioned for it, however.

### Return to Steaua

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Adrian_Neaga&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Return to Steaua")]

After he ended his contract with [Al-Nassr](/wiki/Al-Nassr_FC "Al-Nassr FC"), Neaga returned to [Steaua București](/wiki/FC_Steaua_Bucure%C8%99ti "FC Steaua București"). During the 2003–04 season he scored 13 goals in 15 games.

[Walter Zenga](/wiki/Walter_Zenga "Walter Zenga") was appointed head coach of Steaua București in the summer of 2004 and he soon made Neaga one of the two team leaders, alongside [Mirel Rădoi](/wiki/Mirel_R%C4%83doi "Mirel Rădoi"), and in the first half of that season Neaga played 15 games in [Divizia A](/wiki/Divizia_A "Divizia A"), scoring six goals, while in the [UEFA Cup](/wiki/UEFA_Cup "UEFA Cup") he scored five times in eight games, finishing Steaua's top-scorer in Europe that season.

In February 2005, [Gigi Becali](/wiki/Gigi_Becali "Gigi Becali") received a buying offer from the South Korean club [Chunnam Dragons](/wiki/Jeonnam_Dragons "Jeonnam Dragons") for US$1.5 million. Becali accepted the offer, even though Neaga and the team were due to play a very important game against [Valencia CF](/wiki/Valencia_CF "Valencia CF"), then the [La Liga](/wiki/La_Liga "La Liga") champions and [UEFA Cup](/wiki/UEFA_Cup "UEFA Cup") winners, in the second round of the [UEFA Cup](/wiki/UEFA_Cup "UEFA Cup").

### South Korea

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Adrian_Neaga&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: South Korea")]

During his time at Chunnam Dragons, Neaga was selected in the *Team of the Season* and was awarded the Bronze Boot for finishing third in the goalscorers standings. After 18 months at Chunnam Dragons, Neaga made a statement saying that he would like to return to Steaua București. His club agreed to the transfer, on the condition that the Romanian club pay a US$1.5 million release fee. But Steaua București refused, and Neaga joined Korean club [Seongnam Ilhwa Chunma](/wiki/Seongnam_Ilhwa_Chunma "Seongnam Ilhwa Chunma") instead. With his new team, Neaga won the Korean title for the first time and, also for the first time, played in the [Asian Champions League](/wiki/Asian_Champions_League "Asian Champions League"), scoring a goal in his first game, against [Vietnam](/wiki/Vietnam "Vietnam")'s champions.

### Second return to Steaua București

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Adrian_Neaga&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Second return to Steaua București")]

In June 2007, Neaga returned to Steaua București for a second time. Back at Steaua, he scored a goal in the 2–0 home victory against [BATE Borisov](/wiki/FC_BATE_Borisov "FC BATE Borisov"), thus qualifying for the first time in his life for the [UEFA Champions League](/wiki/UEFA_Champions_League "UEFA Champions League"), where he played against the likes of [Arsenal](/wiki/Arsenal_F.C. "Arsenal F.C.") and [Sevilla](/wiki/Sevilla_FC "Sevilla FC").

In January 2009, Neaga was released by Steaua București and signed for [Azerbaijani](/wiki/Azerbaijan "Azerbaijan") club [Neftchi Baku](/wiki/Neftchi_Baku_PFC "Neftchi Baku PFC").

## Coaching career

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Adrian_Neaga&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Coaching career")]

After he ended his player career, Neaga started a career as coach, and his first job came at the beginning of 2013, when he signed a contract with [Liga IV](/wiki/Liga_IV "Liga IV") team [Unirea Bascov](/wiki/ACS_Unirea_Bascov "ACS Unirea Bascov"). In December 2013, Neaga signed a contract to manage [Atletic Bradu](/wiki/CS_Atletic_Bradu "CS Atletic Bradu"), in [Liga III](/wiki/Liga_III "Liga III"). In July 2014, Neaga signed a contract to coach ACS Urban Titu.

## Honours

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Adrian_Neaga&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Honours")]

**Steaua București**

* [Liga I](/wiki/Liga_I "Liga I"): 2004–05

**Seongnam Ilhwa Chunma**

* [K-League](/wiki/K-League "K-League"): 2006
